feedback.text: Stopping games from becoming lost media is crucial, and in my opinion, this is comparable to burning books without any copies remaining. In 2012 I bought a game called Lego: Universe which is an MMO, meaning massively open multiplayer game that shut down later that year. The community surrounding this game has, after mutiple years managed to get the game running on private servers, in spite of the game being shut down, which doesn't cost the original developers a thing. This is what I wish was true for all games; to require developers of each game that can in the foreseeable future shut down for any reason put work towards giving the resources needed to still operate the game at a capacity which is enough for it to call it a game, not its main menu. Making people pay for these types of games as goods then to have them effectively removed borders on fraud in my opinion. For years, I could not play this game that I had paid for. It is only through the effort of the community surrounding said game that it can be played today. This game had a happy ending which was most personal to me, but on the other end of the spectrum we have Ubisofts The Crew (2014) was permanently disabled after server shutdowns in 2024, leaving players with no offline workaround. Ubisoft selling games like this only to shut them down may already violate Directive 93/13/EEC. What is inherently sad about this is that unlike an MMO the Crew could be enjoyed in a singleplayer session with its story mode; it just required online connectivity for this aswell. I wish this would be added in your list of practices that take control of the consumers online experience, even though it is only in the ball park of these other practises covered in the digital fairness act. Sincerely thank you for reading I hope you have a great day and that you aren't bothered with too much of this in your inbox. With regards, Elias.
